# \[RFC 1\] RFCs \- Design Docs at DoorDash

| *Metadata (will be indexed/searchable). Do not change rows or columns.* |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Author(s):**  |  [Matt Ranney](mailto:matt.ranney@doordash.com) |
| **Status:** | Closed |
| **Origin:** | new |
| **History:** | Drafted: Dec 1, 2022 Opened: Feb 1, 2023 Closed: May 1, 2023	 |
| **Keywords:**  | RFC |
| **References:** |  |

### Background

The purpose of an RFC is to:

* Gather feedback from across the company to improve the design  
* Add visibility to projects to improve cross-org collaboration and reduce waste  
* Produce an artifact for how decisions were made at a point in time

An RFC is **not**:

* An approval mechanism. Teams should have autonomy to solve their own problems.  
* A binding contract. New RFCs can update or obsolete older ones, but the original older ones remain forever.

Why is it called RFCs and not Design Docs? Long ago when computers first started talking to each other, the [IETF](https://www.ietf.org/) was formed. These early networking folks created the process of RFC, “request for comments” as a way to collaborate on designs and make a robust internet. This worked really well, and I think it will improve our velocity by implementing a **process** and **culture** of RFCs at DoorDash. The IETF is developing global standards and protocols, so they understandably have a somewhat slower and more formal process than we’ll need here.

## How Will This Work At DoorDash?

Before people start writing new software, they will write an RFC. This RFC will help the author gather useful feedback.

RFCs will be written in Google Docs and organized in Google Drive at [go/rfc-folder](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1H-BerHhUhgRhEO0pkGEzxfqQnCKI7cap). A single shared folder for all of Engineering will have files that are numbered sequentially. The files will have names like “\[RFC 1\] Design Docs at DoorDash”. With an Eng-wide RFC numbering sequence, RFCs can easily refer to each other, and humans can refer to them easily and unambiguously.

After writing an RFC doc, the author will send it to [rfc@doordash.com](mailto:rfc@doordash.com), a group that interested reviewers [can subscribe to](https://groups.google.com/a/doordash.com/g/rfc). If the author wants to hold a live discussion, they should [sign up for a slot at the weekly RFC review](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1zRVNem2OBZZDlLSFWZ_fPoJFSUWgMhu9P3OVB3vVgTA/edit#gid=133167367) (Wednesdays at 2pm PT) review and let the rfc@ alias (and any other required attendees) know that the topic will be discussed live.

## Giving and Receiving Feedback

There are some interesting dynamics around feedback in a design doc. To make this process effective, here are the norms for RFC feedback: 

* Be respectful  
* Be specific vs. general  
* Be descriptive  
* Be factual  
* Respect the deadlines set by the author  
* If you spot a problem, don’t just point it out \- make an attempt at suggesting a solution, or acknowledge that you don’t have one  
* Not all feedback needs to be “constructive” \- share some positive feedback, too

**The primary purpose of feedback is to assist the RFC author.** As a feedback giver, keep in mind that it can be very easy to fire off a quick comment that could be misunderstood by the author. As a commenter, it’s fine and good to leave a comment about how much you like something, as long as your comment is easily understood as such. Authors like to hear good things from their colleagues about their docs. However it’s important to avoid comments that reflect personal preference or other things that are not helpful to the RFC author. Here is an exaggerated example to make a point:  
![][image1]  
The first one is clearly a message of support, and the second one is some weird grammar thing. But in this case, the second one comes from a senior engineer, so now the author has to try to figure out if this is just fun chit chat or actually pointing out a problem. It’s very easy to add a comment to someone’s doc, but make sure it is going to be **useful** to the RFC author.

**Document authors have complete freedom to do whatever they want with a comment.** Here are some things that an RFC author might do with a comment:

* Resolve it immediately without saying anything. Just as it is easy to leave a comment, it is also easy for the author to resolve one.  
* Add a note saying that they disagree with the comment, then resolving it.  
* Ask a followup question in the comments. Maybe swap comments for a bit if it’s useful to the author.  
* Ask for a followup discussion on Slack or Zoom at the author’s discretion.

While a document author can do whatever they like with the feedback, remember that **good feedback is a gift**. If someone adds a comment pointing something out that wasn’t clear or could use more detail, it’s nice if authors can add a comment like “updated description to clarify this” before they resolve it. 

**Feedback in a doc is not blocking.** It doesn’t matter who wrote the comment or what the comment says. A Google Docs comment thread is not the place to exercise any kind of executive decision making or override or blocking. The author wrote a document to gather feedback, and if as a reader you find something serious that has revealed an important problem with their project, find another channel to exercise that.

## RFC Phases

The phases of an RFC are:

* **Draft** \- a single page describing the problem and proposed solution  
* **Open** \- detailed design for the project  
* **Closed** \- no more edits or comments

### Draft Phase

Authors follow these steps:

1. Write around a page[^1] of text describing the problem, proposed solution, and any other details that commenters might need. Diagrams are not necessary, but fine to use.  
2. Open the Engineering-wide Google Drive folder for RFCs to select the next number, then add the new doc to that folder with the appropriate file name that includes the number, phase, and title.  
3. Set sharing permissions to “Anyone at DoorDash with the link \- Comment”. Now you can refer to your doc as “RFC x”, and people can find it without the link, which is nice.  
4. Email the link to [rfc@doordash.com](mailto:rfc@doordash.com), use the doc title as the subject. Send it around to your team or anyone else that you want to make sure sees it.  
5. Waiting at least **a week for comments** is a good starting point. This might trigger different conversations or meetings, which is great. Document authors are free to adjust the time for the Draft phase as they see fit.  
6. Based on the feedback, the author then decides whether to pursue the idea or close the RFC.

During the draft phase, authors learn about other projects from different teams, different challenges that they might not have considered, and ideally advice from people who have built something like this before. After the initial set of comments are received, the author starts writing the main doc and incorporating this recently received wisdom. Alternatively, the author might decide that this project is not a good idea after all, and then the RFC changes from **draft** to **closed**. In this case the doc is not deleted, but a note should be added that this project was not pursued. Remember that an RFC is not committing the author or their team or the company to a project or timeline, but rather it is a vehicle for feedback and documentation. 

### Open Phase

Assuming the author wants to move forward and describe the full design, they leave the RFC doc in **draft** state while they work on it and disable comments. The RFC is the design doc. After the full design is written into the RFC, diagrams added, and the idea makes sense to the author, it’s time to get another round of comments:

* Change the doc title to reflect the new **open** state  
* Enable comments for anyone at DoorDash with the link  
* Email [rfc@doordash.com](mailto:rfc@doordash.com) with the doc title as the subject. Note that the title includes the same RFC number  
* Wait a week or so for comments and resolve them. Authors can adjust the Open phase comment time as they see fit. See Feedback section above for details on resolving comments.  
* If there are a lot of comments or the idea is particularly contentious, then add this doc to the **RFC Review Calendar**.

After all comments are resolved, move the doc to **closed** state and disable comments.

### Closed Phase

After an RFC doc is **closed** there are no more comments or edits accepted. The author decides for themself when they no longer want more comments, and then they mark the doc as closed. The author and their team can start building the software if they haven’t already.

One thing that frequently happens in software development is that new things like problems or better approaches are learned after engineering work starts. This is normal and expected. When this happens, the author writes a new RFC that **updates** or **obsoletes** the previous one, so go back to the top of this process with a new doc.

Summary of steps for an author:

* Have an idea  
* Write a short “draft” phase RFC doc and claim the next number in the RFC folder.  
* Email [rfc@doordash.com](mailto:rfc@doordash.com) with the link and tell anybody else you want feedback from.  
* Wait a bit. Collect some comments.  
* If you decide to pursue this idea, change your doc to “open” phase and fill in the rest of the details.  
* Email [rfc@doordash.com](mailto:rfc@doordash.com) with the link and anybody else that you want feedback from.  
* Wait a bit. Collect some comments.  
* If you like, schedule a live review on the Weekly RFC Review (will be added to calendars soon)  
* Change the doc state to “closed” and disable comments.

## Origin Types

RFC can refer to each other. Whether an RFC is new or refers to a previous RFC is expressed in the “origin” in the header. An RFC document can have the following origins:

* **new** \- document is not based on any previous RFC  
* **updates X** \- document updates or amends RFC X  
* **obsoletes X** \- document completely replaces RFC X

## Templates

The structure of RFCs is intentionally not specified. The only format that should be in every RFC doc is the header:

| *Metadata (will be indexed/searchable). Do not change rows or columns.* |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Author(s):**  | [Matt Ranney](mailto:matt.ranney@doordash.com) |
| **Status:** | Open |
| **Origin:** | new|updates X|obsoletes X |
| **History:** | Drafted: Dec 1, 2022 Opened: Feb 1, 2023 Closed: May 1, 2023 |
| **Keywords:**  | RFC |
| **References:** |  |

Some authors and teams prefer to use the same structure for their RFCs, which is fine and good. Here are some [Templates You Might Want To Use](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/10_enoPNYSpFdl7haE_XfiSs0tsol2VmR)

## FAQ

**Don’t we already do RFCs?**  
Yes, some people and teams do. Some do not. If they do, usually they only stay within the team that wrote them. It would be more useful if RFCs were both always written and shared broadly.

**Won’t this process slow us down?**  
Maybe a bit at first, but it should result in much more overall velocity. Let’s give it a go and see if it helps.

**Do I have to agree with all of the comments? What do I do if I don’t agree?**  
The author of the doc can do whatever they want with the feedback. It’s probably better if we can get aligned around what we are building, but consensus is not required.

**Is there a template that I have to use?**  
Different teams might have their own templates, but the point here is to get your ideas written down and distributed so that you can get feedback. The RFC folder has some example templates.

**What’s the difference between an RFC and a “design doc”?**  
Nothing. RFCs are how we communicate designs and gather feedback on them. There are many ways that a design doc could be considered different from an RFC, but for the purposes of this process they are called the same thing and handled the same way.

**My team already has an RFC process, do we have to do this one instead?**  
First of all, it's great that you are already doing RFCs\! This eng-wide process here should be additive with whatever your team is doing. We’d like to have all RFCs numbered into a global sequence so RFCs can refer to each other. But if your team wants to curate a folder of RFCs just for your team, can you make a Google Drive shortcut. After you add your RFC to the eng-wide folder, right click on the file and select “Add shortcut to Drive”, then add this to your team’s folder.  
![][image2]

**I cannot find the RFC folder when trying to move my RFC doc. Please help\!**  
Here are the tricks.

1. Go to the [RFCs Folder](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1H-BerHhUhgRhEO0pkGEzxfqQnCKI7cap) and star it![][image3]  
2. You will find the folder under the **Starred** tab when moving a file![][image4]

**How to choose the appropriate RFC Number?**  
Navigate to [https://devconsole.doordash.team/tech-radar/rfcs](https://devconsole.doordash.team/tech-radar/rfcs)**,** pick the next RFC number suggested next to create in Google docs.   
[https://devconsole.doordash.team/tech-radar/rfcs](https://devconsole.doordash.team/tech-radar/rfcs)  
![][image5]  


[^1]:  Pages are for printers. On a computer screen that scrolls and scales we should all be using “pageless” designs. In docs, check File-\>Page setup-\>Pageless.
